text: Hi, my name is Ben and I make movies.
[music] There was a time when I thought
CGI was only getting better. Way back in
2008, I remember seeing Iron Man and was
blown away by how incredibly realistic
his suit looked. It was 100% CGI, but it
also looked like 100% real metal. The
following year, Avatar was released in
theaters, and my mind was blown again by
the stunning detail in the completely
computerenerated world. It was
eyepopping, it was beautiful, and again,
it looked real. Fast forward to 2012 and
I was sitting in an IMAX theater opening
night waiting for The Hobbit, a movie I
had desperately wanted to see ever since
I first experienced The Lord of the
Rings as a kid. But as I watched,
something started to bug me. Middle
Earth looked different. There was
something about the world that felt off.
The orcs, the wargs, the locations,
everything looked too glossy, rubbery,
in a word, fake. Now, there were a lot
of reasons for this decision to shoot in
48 frames per second, the switch from
film to RED cameras, the lack of
practical effects, but the thing that
bothered me the most was how the film
use CGI. Now, don't get me wrong, I'm
not one of those naysayers who thinks
that CGI should never ever under any
circumstances be used. While practical
effects can often look better in various
situations, you need not look any
further than the Avatar movies to see an
entire movie constructed by CGI that
looks fantastic. But The Hobbit did not
look fantastic. In fact, it looked kind
of ugly. Tell me, what looks better,
this or this? What looks more lifelike,
this or [music] this? While the CGI in
the Lord of the Rings is definitely
dated now, I can't help but feel that
the CG creatures in the original movies
feel as though they belong in their
environments much better than those in
The Hobbit. And let's not even compare
the CG goblins to [music] their
practical counterparts. Or for a more
recent example, let's take a look at
Jurassic Park versus [music] Jurassic
World. Which of these feels more real to
you?
[music]
I saw both in IMAX and yet the effects
in the original, now 20 years old, give
me chills. While the newer films with
better technology, often look less
realistic. Is it just me or is CGI
getting worse? With every passing year,
I see more and more movies with fully CG
scenes and creatures that don't just
look unrealistic, they look absolutely
ugly. From fully computerenerated
battles to lackluster creature designs
to fake locations, many films with
incredibly large budgets have incredibly
fake looking effects that [music]
totally take me out of the movie. I
click
on you.
[music]
A good example of this is Black Panther.
A fantastic movie on every level except
for one important detail, the completely
CGI suit. Every time Black Panther is on
screen fighting bad guys, there's no
tension because there's no weight to
him. He doesn't feel like a real person
fighting other real people with real
stakes. Why? Because he's literally a
cartoon, [music] completely
computerenerated, not a stunt man in a
suit. I was able to ignore it for most
of the film, but during the climax, all
of the tension the movie had built up
melted away for me as two cartoon Black
Panthers fought in a completely
computerenerated environment. Again, the
fight had no real weight to it. And by
weight, I don't mean emotional weight. I
mean the characters literally look
weightless. [music] They're
computerenerated puppets, not subject to
the rules of physics. Contrast this to
the earlier scene where Tchala and
Kilmonger fight without their suits on,
and the contrast is huge. In one fight,
I'm on the edge of my seat in suspense.
In the other, I don't feel much of
anything because my brain is
subconsciously telling [music] me that
it's all not actually there. And this
isn't limited to Black Panther. This is
almost every major blockbuster that I've
seen recently. There are, of course,
[music] exceptions to this. The most
recent Planet of the Apes movies have
stunning CGI that's so realistic looking
it's scary. Or take a look at Captain
America: Civil War. Did you realize that
Black Panther's suit in that movie is
also computerenerated? I didn't and I
was shocked when I realized it. So, why
does this look so real and this one
looks so bad? Why do some special
effects from a decade ago look better
than the effects we use today? I did a
lot of research on this and I couldn't
come up with a concrete answer, but from
what I found, I have two theories. Now,
as I really know next to nothing about
creating computerenerated images, feel
free to correct me in the comments below
if I'm wrong or if you have a better
idea. My first theory is that CGI always
looks better when there's a frame of
reference. A lot of what makes CGI seem
so unreal is the lighting and weight of
an object that is being generated. Let's
use Gollum as an example. Gollum was
created using motion capture, but not
always in front of a green screen. Most
times Andy Circus was on set in real
locations interacting with real [music]
actors. This gave the animators a real
person to use as reference. They could
see how the light of the location hit
the real actor, therefore giving them
reference, allowing them to integrate
him into the background better.
Furthermore, it wasn't a weightless
object being created in a computer that
they had to make real, but effects
layered on top of a real person with a
real skeleton that had to interact with
gravity and the environment. This
technique was also used on the Planet of
the Apes movies with the actors loping
around on all fours with [music]
prosthetics on their limbs to give them
the proper length that an ape might
have. This provided the animators with
an ape-like skeleton to animate over,
essentially giving the actors costumes,
just costumes made in a computer as
opposed to fabric. Likewise, the Black
Panther in Civil War was a man in a
costume, just a really crappy looking
costume that allowed the animators to
track the real movements of the actor,
adding a sleeker, better-l looking
costume in post. There have been plenty
[music] of cases of motion capture
characters still looking lackluster, but
I found that this is often when they are
integrated into a computerenerated
location, which can create an uncanny
valley effect, as these frames of
reference are being put into a
completely [music] fabricated location
without real lighting or physics,
causing everything on screen to be
slightly off from what our brains are
familiar with in reality. But not
everything can have a real frame of
reference. If you want to create a giant
spaceship or a massive monster, motion
capture may not be feasible. And
honestly, there have been plenty of
stunning CGI creations from years ago
that did not utilize motion capture that
still hold up and look better than a
whole lot of what we see today. This
leads me to my final theory. Why is CGI
getting worse? Because we have too much
of it. In the original Jurassic [music]
Park, the CG dinosaurs take up only 6
minutes of screen time. The rest is
practical effects. Compare that to
Jurassic World: Fallen Kingdom, which is
chalk full of CG dinosaurs. And now
compare that to every other blockbuster
film coming out this year. DC, The Fast
and Furious, Star Wars, Transformers,
almost every major franchise now is
built off of CGI. Some movies like The
Jungle Book and Avatar and the upcoming
Lion King movie are almost 100%
computerenerated. With so many movies
getting cranked out every year that live
and die on computerenerated effects, is
it any wonder that more and more and
more of them don't look any good? If you
look at older films like The Lord of the
Rings and Jurassic Park, they were made
in a time where CGI wasn't as prevalent.
Both used predominantly practical
effects with computerenerated effects
being used when necessary. This may have
allowed time for the visual effects
artists to refine the effects in the
movies, really dial them in and make
them perfect. Fast forward to our day
and age and there is more of a demand
for visual effects than the industry can
effectively provide. Effects studios are
often underpaid and rushed, forcing them
[music] to crank out thousands of
effects shots on a very short
turnaround. Artists don't really have
the time to perfect effects anymore. On
the flip side, filmmakers are often
given a very short window of time in
which to shoot their films, meaning that
every second on set costs money. This
has promoted an industry that relies
more and more and more heavily on CGI,
even when CGI may not have been totally
necessary. And that's a real shame. We
now have the technology to create
mindblowing visuals the likes of which
previous generations could only have
dreamed of. But it's increasingly rare
to find effects that are not only
vividly realistic, but beautiful to look
at. And I [music] miss it. I miss going
to the movies and getting fully sucked
into new worlds that could not have
possibly existed in reality. Now I get
sucked back into reality all too easily.
For all of the work that goes into these
effects, that's sad. I'm just hoping
that we can get back to that place and
restore a little bit of the magic that
has been missing from the movies.
[music]
So once again, I don't know a lot about
visual effects, so please comment and
let me know if you have more insight on
this topic. I'm really curious about
this and I'd love to learn more. If you
enjoyed this video, please consider
subscribing, sharing, and checking out
my other videos. And as always, thanks
for watching.
